name: Socket.IO
what_it_does: Real-time bidirectional event-based communication library that abstracts WebSocket with rooms, namespaces, auto-reconnection, and long-polling fallback.
use_cases: Build real-time agent-to-client notification channels where the server pushes updates as they occur without client polling
Use rooms to broadcast events to a subset of connected clients (e.g., all agents in a task group) with io.to(room).emit()
Implement multi-agent coordination over a shared namespace where each agent subscribes to relevant event types
Handle unreliable network conditions with automatic client reconnection and buffered event delivery on reconnect
Scaffold a live dashboard that receives streaming updates from a backend agent via socket.on() event listeners
not_for: Environments requiring standard WebSocket protocol compatibility — Socket.IO uses a custom protocol and is NOT interoperable with plain WebSocket clients
Simple one-way streaming from server to client where Server-Sent Events (SSE) would be lighter and simpler
High-frequency binary data streaming (audio, video) where raw WebSocket or WebRTC provides better throughput with less overhead
best_when: Building multi-client real-time applications where room-based broadcasting, auto-reconnect, and fallback transport for unreliable networks are needed.
avoid_when: You need protocol-level WebSocket compatibility with non-Socket.IO clients or are optimizing for minimal connection overhead in a high-scale scenario.

auth.notes: Authentication is implemented via middleware in the connection handshake (io.use()) — tokens or session cookies must be validated manually; no built-in auth scheme.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Connection and disconnect events carry reason strings but detailed error context is limited — agents must implement their own error event handlers per socket.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Event delivery is not guaranteed exactly-once — reconnects can cause duplicate emissions; agents must implement application-level deduplication with event IDs.
known_agent_gotchas: Socket.IO is NOT plain WebSocket — clients built with the ws library or native WebSocket API cannot connect to a Socket.IO server
io.emit() broadcasts to ALL connected clients including the sender; socket.broadcast.emit() sends to all except sender — easy to confuse
Events emitted before the connect event fires are silently dropped unless socket.io client's retries or buffering is configured
Scaling to multiple server instances requires a pub/sub adapter (e.g., socket.io-redis) — without it, rooms only work within a single process
The fallback to HTTP long-polling means connection establishment involves an HTTP upgrade — firewalls/proxies that block WebSocket upgrades may silently degrade to polling without agent awareness

security_score_components.security_notes: Authentication is entirely application-layer — agents must implement token validation in io.use() middleware and handle CORS origins carefully to prevent unauthorized connections.
